records.prompt: Do all sections of the text follow the same arc (problem → evidence → anecdote → advice, or hook → claim → evidence → close)? Human writers vary section structure; AI defaults to a uniform shape across every section.

values: every section follows the same arc

flagged_when: every section follows the same arc

records.prompt: Does the whole text sit in one register (formal, academic-accessible, warm-but-authoritative), or does the writer drift between registers — formal to colloquial, careful to loose, sincere to wry? Tonal uniformity is the consistency itself, not any particular register.

Are the "specific" examples actually specific (someone's lived detail with names, dates, places, sensory grounding), or genre-convention filler that anyone could have written ("the smell of coffee on a Sunday morning", "the way light hits the kitchen table")? List specific phrases that perform specificity without achieving it.

Does the text commit to a position, or default to "balanced" treatment of everything? Look for "there are compelling arguments on both sides" framing where the original had a clear stance. Surface false-balance phrasing is partly covered by `no-false-concession-hedges` (#23a); this item flags the broader stance erasure.

Is technical vocabulary clumped (the writer is in the weeds where they actually know things, plain prose elsewhere) or spread evenly across the piece (suspicious — humans don't distribute jargon uniformly)?

values: jargon clumps where the writer knows things
jargon spreads uniformly across the text

flagged_when: jargon spreads uniformly across the text

records.prompt: Are there forced cross-modal metaphors — emotions get tastes, sounds get colours, abstract concepts get textures — that don't earn their grounding? Real synesthetic writing is specific and grounded ("a great plateful of blue water" — Woolf had stood before a view and sat down to a meal). AI synesthesia is unanchored. List specific phrases that map senses unjustifiably.

Are the metaphors plausible-but-anyone's ("learning the ukulele is like teaching your fingers to dance again"), or do they come from the writer's specific experience ("the first week my fingers couldn't stretch far enough for a G chord")? List specific metaphors that draw from the statistical middle rather than personal grounding.

First detect the genre of the text (academic, student_essay, poetry, fiction, or default if none of these fit). Then run the genre's watchlist below. Report `Genre detected: <genre>.` plus either watchlist findings or "Watchlist coverage pending." if the genre's watchlist is empty.

academic.description: Verify citations, DOIs, dates, journals, reference order, and whether cited works actually support the claim. Plausible citation format is not evidence.

student_essay.description: Watch for rubric language echoing back ("the author creates a tone", "I can tell because", "this quote shows that"). Common in AI-generated student essays.

poetry.description: Watch for default quatrains, unrequested rhyme, first-person plural overuse, mood-word accumulation, and formal gestures that do not follow through.

fiction.description: Watch for flattened dialogue, "as-you-know" exposition, parenthetical stage directions, locked POV with no pressure, over-resolved endings, and scene pacing that never surprises.

default.description: No genre-specific watchlist applies. The agent reports "Genre detected: default. No genre-specific findings." and moves on.
